title: Rachel
content:
혻혻혻혻I

혻혻혻혻In paris all look占폻 hot and like to fade.
혻혻혻혻Brown in the garden of the Tuileries,
혻혻혻혻Brown with September, droop占폻 the chestnut-trees.
혻혻혻혻占폯was dawn; a brougham roll占폻 through the streets, and made

혻혻혻혻Halt at the white and silent colonnade
혻혻혻혻Of the French Theatre. Worn with disease,
혻혻혻혻Rachel, with eyes no gazing can appease,
혻혻혻혻Sate in the brougham, and those blank walls survey占폻.

혻혻혻혻She follows the gay world, whose swarms have fled
혻혻혻혻To Switzerland, to Baden, to the Rhine;
혻혻혻혻Why stops she by this empty play-house drear?

혻혻혻혻Ah, where the spirit its highest life hath led,
혻혻혻혻All spots, match占폻 with that spot, are less divine;
혻혻혻혻And Rachel占퐏 Switzerland, her Rhine, is here!

혻혻혻혻II

혻혻혻혻Unto a lonely villa in a dell
혻혻혻혻Above the fragrant warm Provencal shore
혻혻혻혻The dying Rachel in a chair they bore
혻혻혻혻Up the steep pine-plumed paths of the Estrelle,

혻혻혻혻And laid her in a stately room, where fell
혻혻혻혻The shadow of a marble Muse of yore
혻혻혻혻The rose-crown占폻 queen of legendary lore,
혻혻혻혻Polymnia, full on her death-bed. 占폯was well!

혻혻혻혻The fret and misery of our northern towns,
혻혻혻혻In this her life占퐏 last day, our poor, our pain,
혻혻혻혻Our jangle of false wits, our climate占퐏 frowns,

혻혻혻혻Do for this radiant Greek-soul占폻 artist cease;
혻혻혻혻Sole object of her dying eyes remain
혻혻혻혻The beauty and the glorious art of Greece.

혻혻혻혻III

혻혻혻혻Sprung from the blood of Israel占퐏 scatter占폻 race,
혻혻혻혻At a mean inn in German Aarau born,
혻혻혻혻To forms from antique Greece and Rome uptorn,
혻혻혻혻Trick占폻 out with a Parisian speech and face,

혻혻혻혻Imparting life renew占폻, old classic grace;
혻혻혻혻Then soothing with thy Christian strain forlorn,
혻혻혻혻A-Kempis! her departing soul outworn,
혻혻혻혻While by her bedside Hebrew rites have place

혻혻혻혻Ah, not the radiant spirit of Greece alone
혻혻혻혻She had one power, which made her breast its home!
혻혻혻혻In her, like us, there clash占폻, contending powers,

혻혻혻혻Germany, France, Christ, Moses, Athens, Rome.
혻혻혻혻The strife, the mixture in her soul, are ours;
혻혻혻혻Her genius and her glory are her own.
meaning:
Matthew Arnold's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Rachel"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
